title: Hardware Interrupts & NAPI
description: How the NIC signals the CPU that packets have arrived, and how NAPI polling reduces interrupt overhead.
fullExplanation: When a NIC receives a packet, it needs to notify the CPU. The traditional method is a hardware interrupt (IRQ) — the NIC raises an interrupt line, the CPU stops what it's doing and runs the interrupt handler. At low packet rates this is fine. At high rates (millions of packets/second), the CPU spends all its time handling interrupts — interrupt livelock. NAPI (New API) solves this: after the first interrupt, the NIC is told to stop interrupting and the kernel polls the ring buffer in a loop until it's empty, then re-enables interrupts.

interruptTypes.description: NIC writes to a special memory address to signal interrupt — more scalable than legacy IRQ lines
Multiple MSI vectors — one per RX/TX queue. Enables multi-queue NICs to use multiple CPU cores.
Shared interrupt line — all devices on same IRQ compete. Legacy, avoid on modern hardware.

napiFlow.description: Interrupt handler runs — disables NIC interrupts, schedules NAPI poll
NAPI poll runs — processes up to budget packets from ring buffer
If ring empty: re-enable interrupts, exit poll
If budget exhausted: yield CPU, reschedule poll (prevents starvation)

keyFacts: NAPI budget: default 64 packets per poll cycle (net.core.netdev_budget)
Interrupt coalescing: delay interrupt until N packets or T microseconds — reduces interrupt rate
ethtool -c eth0: show interrupt coalescing settings
ethtool -C eth0 rx-usecs 50: coalesce interrupts up to 50µs — reduces CPU at cost of latency
RSS (Receive Side Scaling): MSI-X + multiple queues — spread interrupts across CPU cores
RPS (Receive Packet Steering): software RSS for NICs without hardware multi-queue
/proc/interrupts: show interrupt counts per CPU per device
